coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots enhance anticancer activity of cisplatin via increasing its cellular and nuclear uptake
coredata.dc:creator: Sui, Xin
Luo, Chao
Wang, Chong
Zhang, Fangwei
Zhang, Jingyan
Guo, Shouwu
coredata.dc:description: Using assistant reagents to improve the pharmaceutical performance of cisplatin (CDDP) has been one of promising strategies. Owing to their extraordinary properties, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have shown great potentials in biomedical applications. Here we demonstrate that the combination of GQDs with CDDP can effectively enhance the cytotoxicity, cell cycle arrest, and DNA fragmentation that induced by CDDP. We also found that the GQDs improved the interaction of CDDP with DNA. Combing these results with our previous finding that graphene oxide could enhance the permeability of the cells, we believe GQDs improve the pharmaceutical performance of CDDP via first increasing its cellular uptake through improving the cell permeability, and then reinforcing its interaction with DNA once it is inside cells. The remarkable enhancement to CDDP cellular uptake in CDDP resistant cells further specifies that GQDs can increase chemotherapy efficacy of anticancer drugs that are suboptimal due to the drug resistance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cisplatin
Graphene quantum dots
Anticancer activity
Cell permeability
Cellular uptake

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Pharmacology, Oncology, Drug Delivery, Cellular Uptake, Cisplatin, Graphene quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Platinum amount inside cell and nucleus
Cell cycle analysis by flow cytometry
GQDs intensify the cytotoxicity of CDDP
GQDs reinforce the CDDP effect to cell cycles
GQDs increase the apoptosis bodies and DNA fragmentation induced by CDDP
GQDs enhance the interaction between CDDP and DNA
GQDs boost cellular and nuclear uptake of Pt by increasing the cell permeability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cell viability of the cancer cells treated with CDDP alone, GQDs alone and CDDP/GQDs for 48h. (A) MCF-7 cells, (B) A549 cells, (C) Hela cells, and (D) MGC-803 cells. The concentrations of GQDs and CDDP were labeled on the top and bottom x-axes. The y-values represented the percentages of the viable cells, with the control cells regarded as 100%, they are mean values of triplicate experiments.

Effects of GQDs on the activity of CDDP. (A) MCF-7 cell cycles without and with pre-treatment with GQDs, CDDP, and CDDP/GQDs for 24h, respectively. CDDP was 15μM for all samples, GQDs was 30μgmL−1 in the sample with GQDs alone. GQDs in CDDP/GQDs1 were 7.5 and 30μgmL−1 in CDDP/GQDs2. (B) Percentages for each phase of the cell cycles are summarized in the table. (C) Fluorescence microscopy images of MCF-7 cells after treatment with CDDP, CDDP/GQDs, and GQDs for 9h, respectively. The concentrations of CDDP and GQDs are the same as in (A). The nuclei were stained with DAPI. Scale bar: 25μm. (D) DNA fragmentation of the MCF-7 cells treated with GQDs, CDDP, and CDDP/GQDs for 24h. CDDP was 20μM, the control of GQDs was 40μgmL−1, GQDs were 10 and 40μgmL−1 in CDDP/GQDs3 and CDDP/GQDs4, respectively.

Interaction of GQDs with CDDP. (A) Effect of GQDs to the reaction product of CDDP with OPDA (absorption at 750nm). GQDs were 0 (blue line), 10 (green line), 50 (pink line), and 80μgmL−1 (brown line). The concentration of CDDP was 10μM, OPDA was 1.4mgmL-1. Reaction time was 4h (37°C). (B) Agarose gel (1%) image of the pBR322 plasmid DNA (0.4μg) incubated with GQDs, CDDP, and CDDP/GQDs for 24h (37°C), respectively. The concentration of CDDP was 15μM, GQDs were 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, and 50μgmL−1 from lanes 4-11, lanes 12-19, respectively.

(A) Gel image of the PCR products with the presence of CDDP, GQDs, and their combination, as indicated on the top of the image. (B) Comparison of yields of the PCR products obtained from the gel intensities of (A). The values (mean of triplicate experiments) represent the amount of PCR products, with the control as 1.
Fluorescence microscopy images of the MCF-7 cells that were stained with PI (10μgmL−1) for 3min and Rho123 (1μM) for 5min after the incubation with 50μgmL−1 of GQDs for 6h. Scale bar: 25μm.
Fluorescence microscopy images of the A549 cells treated with GQDs (or GO, 30μgmL−1), CDDP (15μM), the combination of CDDP with GQDs, and with GO for 9h. Scale bar: 25μm.

Proposed mechanism that the GQDs enhance the activity of CDDP (two blue pathways, thinker blue line represents the primary pathway). The black colored pathways are generally recognized uptake of free CDDP by cells. 1

Cellular and nuclear accumulation of CDDP with and without GQDs in MCF-7 cells obtained from ICP-MS. Cellular platinum was normalized to cellular protein. Nuclear Pt was normalized to nuclear DNA in mass.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This research was carried out with financial support from the national “973 Program” of China (Nos. 2015CB931801, 2014CB260411), National Science foundation of China (No. 11374205), the state key laboratory of bioreactor engineering (No. 2060204), 111 Project (No. B07023), the Shanghai Committee of Science and Technology (Nos. 11DZ2260600), and national “863” Program of China (No. 2012AA022603) for financial support of this work..

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots enhance anticancer activity of cisplatin via increasing its cellular and nuclear uptake

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bioreactor Engineering, Shanghai Key Laboratory of New Drug Design, School of Pharmacy, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai, P.R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bioreactor Engineering, Shanghai Key Laboratory of New Drug Design, School of Pharmacy, East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronic Engineering, School of Electronic Information and Electrical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, P.R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electronic Engineering, School of Electronic Information and Electrical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: J. Zhang, State Key Laboratory of Bioreactor Engineering, Shanghai Key Laboratory of New Drug Design, School of Pharmacy, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200237, P.R. China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bioreactor Engineering, Shanghai Key Laboratory of New Drug Design, School of Pharmacy, East China University of Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: S. Guo, Department of Electronic Engineering, School of Electronic Information and Electrical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, 200240, P.R. China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electronic Engineering, School of Electronic Information and Electrical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using assistant reagents to improve the pharmaceutical performance of cisplatin (CDDP) has been one of promising strategies. Owing to their extraordinary properties, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have shown great potentials in biomedical applications. Here we demonstrate that the combination of GQDs with CDDP can effectively enhance the cytotoxicity, cell cycle arrest, and DNA fragmentation that induced by CDDP. We also found that the GQDs improved the interaction of CDDP with DNA. Combing these results with our previous finding that graphene oxide could enhance the permeability of the cells, we believe GQDs improve the pharmaceutical performance of CDDP via first increasing its cellular uptake through improving the cell permeability, and then reinforcing its interaction with DNA once it is inside cells. The remarkable enhancement to CDDP cellular uptake in CDDP resistant cells further specifies that GQDs can increase chemotherapy efficacy of anticancer drugs that are suboptimal due to the drug resistance.
Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can enhance the anti-cancer activity of cisplatin through increasing its cellular and nuclear uptake, and enhancing its interaction with DNA.

ce:sections.ce:para: Cisplatin (cis-diamminedichloroplatinum (II), CDDP) is one of effective chemotherapeutic agents that widely used in cancer treatment, especially for testicular cancer, and the cure rate for it is greater than 90%. 1,2 Hundreds of derivatives of CDDP thus have been tested for their anticancer properties. 3,4 However, among these complexes, only cisplatin, oxaliplatin, and carboplatin received world-wide approval for clinical use. 5 The therapeutic performance of the platinum complexes is severely affected by their side effects and inherent and acquired resistances. 1 Several mechanisms of CDDP resistance including reduction of CDDP accumulation by changing the profile of uptake/efflux, inactivation of CDDP by sulphur-containing molecules, and reparation of CDDP adducts have been proposed. 1,6 One effective avenue is to employ new delivery strategies to bypass cisplatin resistance. 5 For examples, a platinum prodrug conjugated to a gold-nano rod-based delivery agent is taken up by cells through endocytosis, avoiding the resistance-associated to the uptake mediated by the copper transport protein Ctr1. 7 The use of nanoscale metal-organic frameworks for the co-delivery of CDDP and pooled small interfering RNAs (siRNAs) enhance the therapeutic efficacy by silencing multiple drug resistance (MDR) genes and re-sensitizing resistant ovarian cancer cells to CDDP treatment. 8 In addition, the synergistic release of CDDP and O2 that achieved in an H2O2-responsive nano-carrier incorporated with catalase combined the advantages of chemotherapy and oxygen therapy, and also demonstrated improved therapeutic efficacy against CDDP resistant cell lines. 9 Many CDDP delivery systems have been developed, but only Lipoplatin, in which cisplatin is formulated into liposomes, has gone through clinical trial, and has shown equal effectiveness to CDDP and documented a simultaneous lowering of the side effects of CDDP. 10,11

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a single atomic carbon layer with lateral size smaller than 100nm, owing to their exceptional physicochemical properties, biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and small lateral size, have gained lots of attention for biomedical applications, including biosensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery. 12-17 To name some, GQDs passivated with PEG (~15nm) are demonstrated to be a good candidate for drug carrier, which has been shown to possess good drug loading capacity (2.5mg/mg of doxorubicin at pH7.4). 18 GQDs anchored with hyaluronic acid (HA) moieties could delivery doxorubicin (DOX) targeted to CD44-overexpressed tumors; and HA-GQDs are able to load∼75% of DOX. 19 Our group previously found that GQDs could efficiently deliver DOX to the nucleus through DOX/GQD conjugates, and improved cytotoxicity of DOX dramatically. 20 Furthermore, the DOX/GQD conjugates could also increase the nuclear uptake and cytotoxicity of DOX to drug-resistant cancer cells indicating that the DOX/GQD conjugates may be capable to increase chemotherapy efficacy of anticancer drugs that are suboptimal due to the drug resistance. 20 Besides that, we previously found that GQDs could make the copper complexes containing labile ligands showing nuclease activity. 21,22 The labile chloride ligand of the copper complexes was readily replaced by the peripheral carboxylic groups of the GQDs forming a conjugate. Thus an electron-transfer from the electron-rich GQDs to the copper complexes occurred, which promoted the reduction of copper ions and accelerated their reaction with O2 forming superoxide anions and copper-centered radicals. These active species can oxidize DNA molecules, indicates that the GQD/copper complex conjugates possess DNA cleavage activity. 21

On the basis of these findings, here we investigate the effect of GQDs on the anticancer activity of CDDP. The hypothesis is that the structure of CDDP is similar to the copper complexes with two labile ligands that we studied, 21 thus the GQD possibly can act as a carrier to CDDP. Also, the combination of CDDP with GQDs may enhance the interaction of CDDP with its primary target DNA due to the intercalation of GQDs with DNA. 16,20,21 Furthermore, the interaction of GQDs to cells may also help the cellular uptake of CDDP, which may eventually affect CDDP’s efficacy as well.

ce:section.ce:para: Agarose gel electrophoresis was carried out with a DYY-6C electrophoresis apparatus (Liuyi Instrumental Co., China). The agarose gels were visualized and digitized with the FR-200A gel image analysis system and analyzed by Smart View software. The UV–vis measurements were performed on a Cary 50 spectrometer (Varian, USA). Fluorescence images of cells were obtained by a fluorescence microscopy (Nikon ECLIPSE, Ti-S). Flow cytometry was performed on FACS Calibur (BD Biosciences, Mountain View, CA).

All reagents and organic solvents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. CDDP was obtained from TCI (Tokyo Chemical Industry, Japan). All of the other chemicals and reagents were obtained from Sino-Pharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. China. Unless specifically indicated, CDDP was stored at a concentration of 1mM in pure water. GQDs were prepared through photo-Fenton reactions of graphene oxide and were characterized by AFM, XPS, TEM, and FT-IR, UV-visible, and fluorescence spectroscopies as described in our previous work. 16 The aqueous suspension of GQDs was stored at room temperature, and sonicated, filtered with a sterilized 0.22μm filter prior to use.

The following protocol is an adaptation of the established procedures. 36 1.5mg of CDDP was dissolved in 10mL of 10−4 M HCl solution to prepare CDDP stock solution. O-phenylenediamine (OPDA) stock solution was prepared with 14mg of OPDA dissolving in 10mL of DMF. 30μL CDDP stock solution was mixed with different concentration of GQDs with a final volume of 100μL at 37°C for 4h, followed by the addition of 100μL OPDA stock solution. The mixture was heated for 30min at 90°C, then allowed to cool, and the total volume was completed to 1mL with a mixture of DMF and water (7/3, v/v). The absorbance of the product at 705nm was monitored.

The pBR322 plasmid DNA (0.2μgμL−1) was incubated with different concentrations of CDDP and GQDs at 37°C for 24h in HEPES buffer (50mM, pH7.4). The mixtures were underwent electrophoresis in 1% agarose gel containing ethidum bromide (0.5mgmL−1) in TBE buffer (45mM Tris-borate, 1mM EDTA, pH8.0) at 100V for 45min.

Similar to PCR experiment, except that template pBR 322 plasmid DNA was pre-treated with different concentrations of CDDP, or CDDP with GQDs at 37°C for 2h. The PCR reaction mixture is comprised of 1.5mM of MgCl2; 200μM of dNTP; 0.2μM of the primers; 1.5 U of Taq DNA polymerase (Thermo). The sequences of the primers were (R) 5’- CACGGAAACCGAAGACCA -3’ and (F) 5’-CGCTAACGGATTCACCAC-3. DNA (Sangon Biotech, China, 1μL). The final volume of the mixture is 25μL. The PCR cycle is 95°C for 30s, 52°C for 30s, and 72°C for 30s for 35cycles performed in a Bio-radT100 thermal cycler. The reaction product was examined with 1% agarose gel.

Human breast cancer cells MCF-7, lung cancer A549 cells, cervical cancer HeLa cells, gastric cancer MGC-803 cells, hepatic cancer SMMC-7721 cells, liver normal L02 cells, liver normal QSG-7701 cells, and human bronchial epithelial 16HBE cells (purchased from Shanghai Cell Bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences, China) were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% antibiotics (penicillin and streptomycin) at 37°C under 5% CO2. The CDDP resistant cell A549/DDP cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium with 10% fetal bovine serum, 1% antibiotics (penicillin and streptomycin) and 0.1μgmL−1 of CDDP at 37°C under 5% CO2. The viability of MCF-7, A549, HeLa, MGC-803, A549/DDP, SMMC-7721 and L02 cells in the presence of CDDP or CDDP/GQDs was studied using MTT assay kit (Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology, China). Cells were plated in a 96-well plates at a density of 10,000-15,000 cells. Background control wells containing the same volume of complete culture medium were included in each assay. The microplate was incubated for overnight at 37°C. Then the cells were washed with PBS buffer and incubated with CDDP and GQDs in serum-free medium, and the plate was incubated further for 48h. The optical density of DMSO solution of formazan at 490nm was recorded after 4h of incubation with MTT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Platinum amount inside cell and nucleus
ce:section.ce:para: The MCF-7 cells were treated with 50μgmL−1 GO or GQDs for 3h. Then the media was decanted, the cells were washed twice with PBS buffer, and then 20μM CDDP were added. After 3h incubation, the media was decanted, the cell monolayer washed with 3mL PBS, and the cells lysed with 800μL of 1% SDS. The cell lysate was further lysed by sonication on a Qsonica Ultrasonic processor for 10s at 20% amplitude; 750μL of the lysate was then combined with 750μL of a 2% HNO3 (v/v) solution, while the remainder of the lysate was quantified for protein by BCA assay. 23 The 1% HNO3 solution was analyzed for platinum content on PerkinElmer NexIon 300x ICP-MS. Platinum counts were normalized to the amount of protein determined from the BCA analysis (to obtain [platinum]/[protein] values). Standard errors for three independent experiments are shown.

Similar to the experiment of determining the Pt amount inside cells, for the nuclear Pt concentration measurement, the treated cells were lysed with 500μL of Dzup Genomic DNA Isolation Reagent (Sango Biotech). After ethanol precipitation and two ethanol washes, the DNA was dissolved in 5mL of water. The DNA in the solution was quantified by the absorbance at 260nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell cycle analysis by flow cytometry
ce:section.ce:para: The CDDP, GQDs, and CDDP/GQDs treated cells were digested with trypsin, washed twice with chilled PBS and centrifuged. The cell pellets were re-suspended in 70% ethanol at −20°C overnight. The cells were centrifuged at 1,000rpm for 5min; the pellet was washed twice with chilled PBS, suspended in 500μL of PBS, and incubated with RNase (final concentration 100μgmL−1) and PI (50μgmL−1) at 37°C for 30min. A minimum of 10,000 cells per sample were counted for cell cycle analysis and the DNA histograms were further analyzed using ModFit software.

The CDDP, GQDs, and CDDP/GQDs treated cells were washed twice with PBS buffer to remove free CDDP and GQDs and then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde (pH=7.4) for 15min. The cells were then washed twice with PBS buffer to remove the residual fixing reagent. To stain the nuclei, 300μL of DAPI in PBS solution (2μgmL−1) was added and incubated for 5min. MCF-7 cells with and without pretreatment with GQDs or GO were washed twice with PBS buffer and stained with PI (10μgmL−1) for 3min or Rhodamine 123 in PBS solution (1μM) for 5min. Before the fluorescence imaging, cells were washed twice with PBS. Fluorescence images were taken with a Nikon ECLIPSE Ti-S microscope.

The CDDP, GQDs, and CDDP/GQDs treated cells (24h) were lysed with 500μL of Dzup Genomic DNA Isolation Reagent (Sango Biotech, China). After ethanol precipitation and two ethanol washes were performed, the DNA was dissolved in 80μL of water. The solution was quantified for DNA by the measurement of absorbance at 260nm and examined for DNA fragmentation on a 1% agarose gel electrophoresis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs intensify the cytotoxicity of CDDP
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of CDDP in combination with GQDs was first tested using several human cancer cell lines, including breast adenocarcinoma MCF-7 cells, lung adenocarcinoma A549 cells, HeLa cells and gastric adenocarcinoma MGC-803 cells. GQDs alone generally exhibited relatively low toxicity to the tested cell lines (Figure 1 , blue lines). 15,19,20,25,24 CDDP alone exhibited cytotoxicity to MCF-7, A549, HeLa, and MGC-803 cells as reported in the literatures as shown Figure 1 (black lines). 26-29 In the presence of GQDs, cytotoxicity of CDDP to the cell lines was enhanced. The treatment with CDDP for 48h alone decreased the cell survival rate in a dose-dependent manner, 50% of the MCF-7 cells were killed at CDDP concentration of 5μM. Interestingly, the addition of 10μgmL−1 of GQDs greatly enhanced the killing ability of CDDP, nearly 75% of the MCF-7 cells were killed under the same CDDP concentration, slight higher than the sum of the cytotoxicity of CDDP and GQDs. Similarly, the combination of CDDP with GQDs dramatically enhanced the killing to A549 cells, as well. The killing rate increased from approximately 10% with 8μM of CDDP to 50% in the presence of 16μgmL−1 of GQDs, and increased from 40% to 70% at 14μM of CDDP in the presence of 28μgmL−1 of GQDs, although for A549 cells, the cytotoxicity of GQDs themselves is higher. Similar effect was observed in HeLa and MGC-803 cells. Apparently, the combination of CDDP and GQDs could increase the cytotoxicity of CDDP, and the cytotoxicity is the sum of the toxicities of CDDP and GQDs. In fact, the cytotoxicity of the combination of CDDP and GQDs to a normal hepatic cell line (L02), QSG-7701, and 16HBE cells was investigated as shown in Figure 1Sb and Sc. Almost no effect was observed to these normal cells upon the combination of GQDs neither at high or low GQDs concentrations, but it did affect hepatic cancer cell line SMMC-7721 (Figure 1Sa). However, it is not clear why GQDs do not affect these normal cells, though it is a good sign. In addition, we just selected several normal cell lines in hand, they cannot represent all types of normal cell lines. Therefore it is inappropriate to draw a conclusion that GQDs enhance CDDP delivery to cancerous cells selectively. It is possible that certain type cells are not affected by GQDs no matter they are normal or cancerous cells, this, however, definitely needs further systemic cellular level studies. Figure S2 showed that the ratio between GQDs and CDDP was chosen as 1μM to 2μgmL−1, because there is no dramatic change in the cytotoxicity when further increasing the ratio. As a matter of fact, excess CDDP was removed via dialysis during the sample preparation, and the ratio of CDDP to GQDs in the final sample was found to be 8.2μM to 16μgmL−1. Therefore this ratio was maintained in the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs reinforce the CDDP effect to cell cycles
ce:section.ce:para: One of the generally accepted mechanisms of the CDDP cytotoxicity is believed through influencing cell cycles. 30 Therefore, to further understand the GQDs enhancement to CDDP cytotoxicity, cell cycle analysis was conducted in the presence of GQDs and CDDP. MCF-7 cells were treated with different concentrations of CDDP and GQDs for 24h, and the treated cells were fixed with 75% ethanol, stained with propidium iodide (PI) and analyzed by flow cytometry. As shown in Figure 2 , A and B, upon 15μM of CDDP treatment, 20.9±0.7% of the cells were arrested at the G2/M phase, and this fraction is much higher than that of the untreated cells (12.6±0.8%). In the presence of 7.5 and 30μgmL−1 of GQDs in addition to 15μM of CDDP, the cells arrested at the G2/M phase were 23.9±0.8% and 26.3±0.6%, respectively. The fraction of the cells that arrested at G2/M phase increased with the increase of the concentration of GQDs. It was proposed that G2 arrest was essential to the process of engaging cell death with CDDP treatment. 31 Though the detailed mechanism of CDDP on cell cycles has not been fully understood, the presence of GQDs obviously enhanced the intrinsic effect of CDDP on cell cycles, which is in along with their impact on the cytotoxicity of CDDP (Figure 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs increase the apoptosis bodies and DNA fragmentation induced by CDDP
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of the GQDs on the cytotoxicity and cell cycles that caused by CDDP is possible displayed in other biological activities of the cells, for example, apoptosis, since it has been well documented that CDDP triggers apoptosis. 32,33 The apoptosis bodies (round and/or shrunken nuclei in the DAPI-stained cells) and DNA fragmentation that often observed in the cells undergoing apoptosis were thus examined with the CDDP and GQDs together treated MCF-7 cells. 34,35 As shown in Figure 2, C, after the treatment with 15μM of CDDP for 9h, no apoptosis body was observed. With 30μgmL−1 of GQDs alone, there are little apoptosis bodies observed. (Figure 2, C). However, in the presence of combination of 15μM of CDDP and 7.5μgmL−1 of GQDs, apoptosis bodies showed up noticeably, which is similar to the result of the incubation with high concentration of CDDP alone (Figure S3a). The number of apoptosis bodies increased with the increase of GQDs concentration (Figure 2, C), indicating that the GQDs significantly reinforced the apoptosis induced by CDDP. Similar results were obtained with A549 cells with a higher CDDP concentration (Figure S3b).

To further examine the effect that GQDs generated with CDDP, DNA was isolated from the cells that were treated with GQDs, CDDP, and CDDP/GQDs, respectively. The extracted DNA were analyzed by agarose gel electrophoresis. As shown in Figure 2, D, no DNA fragment was observed in the control sample. There is no very dramatic DNA fragmentation observed in the CDDP treated sample either, but comparing with the control sample, the DNA band in the CDDP treated sample apparently tailed longer. In addition, the major DNA bands move much forward than the control. We believe these are indications of slight DNA damage induced by CDDP. In the GQDs treated sample, there is little fragmentation observed. However, in the samples treated with CDDP and GQDs, DNA fragmentation was increased apparently comparing to the cells treated with CDDP or GQDs alone, as evidenced by a long bright tail and fast-moved DNA band in the gel. DNA fragments appeared to be more evident with the increasing of GQDs concentration (lanes CDDP/GQDs3 vs CDDP/GQDs4). The DNA damage result further confirmed that GQDs can enhance the activity of CDDP, which is in along with the increase of apoptosis bodies in the nuclei (Figure 2, C). Together, these results revealed that GQDs could boost the cytotoxicity, cell cycle arrest and apoptosis that induced by CDDP.

To understand these effects of GQDs on the performance of CDDP, the interactions between CDDP and GQDs, and its target DNA molecules were further investigated at molecular level. Interaction between CDDP and GQDs was first examined via UV–vis spectroscopy in vitro. The assay is based on the ligand exchange reaction of CDDP with o-phenylenediamine (OPDA), 36 where CDDP and OPDA form a complex giving a maximum absorbance at 705nm that can be used to monitor the concentration change of CDDP. Figure 3 , A showed the UV–vis spectra of the reaction product of CDDP with OPDA in the presence of different amount of GQDs. There is no interaction between GQDs and OPDA (Figure 3, A, red line) as evidenced by the unchanged UV-visible spectrum of OPDA after the addition of GQDs. However, upon the addition of different concentrations of GQDs, 0 (blue line), 10 (green line), 50 (pink line), and 80μgmL−1 (brown line), to the mixture of CDDP and OPDA, respectively; the absorbance of the mixture at 705nm declined dramatically with the increase of GQDs. Since there is no interaction between GQDs and OPDA, the intensity decrease at 705nm is apparently caused by the interaction between GQDs and CDDP, which eventually decreases the interaction between CDDP and OPDA. This is expected, because as mentioned earlier, there are peripheral carboxylic groups in the GQDs, the labile chloride ligands of CDDP can be replaced by carboxylic groups of GQDs forming a CDDP/GQDs complex as in the case of the copper complexes with GQDs. 21 The formation of CDDP/GQDs complexes was also partially supported by their FT-IR spectra, in which the peak at 3285cm−1 that assigned to N-H vibration of amino groups of CDDP, and the peak at 1750cm−1 that assigned to C=O vibration of carboxyl groups were both observed (Figure S4). Though the shifts of the peaks are small, and FT-IR spectra also cannot provide a quantitative number of CDDP bound to one GQD, it is clear that in the CDDP/GQDs sample, both components are existed. However, in terms of chemistry, CDDP/GQDs complexes may not be very stable as GQDs/copper complexes, 21 because generally platinum has a preference to nitrogen and heavy donor atoms such as sulfur, but has low affinity to oxygen atoms. 37 Nevertheless, the interaction between GQDs and CDDP indeed exists.

The interaction between CDDP with GQDs is further confirmed by their interaction with DNA molecules. The binding of CDDP to DNA produces a more compact DNA structure, leading to an increase in electrophoretic mobility of the plasmid DNA (lane 2 vs lane 1, Figure 3, B). 38 The binding of CDDP to DNA also made the EB staining less efficient, thus the DNA bands faded comparing with the control sample. Upon addition of different concentrations of GQDs (Lanes 4-11), the fast electrophoretic mobility of DNA induced by the CDDP binding was maintained, revealing that though GQDs interact with CDDP, CDDP still interact with DNA. Under the same concentrations, GQDs alone would not change the migration of DNA as shown in lanes 12-19. However, at the present, whether CDDP alone and/or CDDP/GQDs together bind to DNA remains unclear.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs enhance the interaction between CDDP and DNA
ce:section.ce:para: It has been generally accepted that CDDP forms a covalent adduct with DNA resulting in both intra- and inter-strand crosslinks, which inhibits DNA replication and RNA transcription. 39 If CDDP interacts with DNA molecules as a CDDP/GQDs complex, the covalent binding of CDDP to DNA may be affected. Unexpectedly, a PCR stop assay with CDDP alone, CDDP and GQDs performed with plasmid pBR322 as a template, displayed an opposite result. The plasmid pBR322 was pre-incubated with different amounts of GQDs, CDDP, and CDDP/GQDs, respectively; then was replicated using a regular PCR procedure. 40 The PCR products were examined with gel electrophoresis (Figure 4 , A), and the PCR yields were determined by the intensity of the gel bands (Figure 4, B). GQDs alone under this condition do not affect PCR. 41 CDDP alone induced inhibition to PCR in a concentration-dependent manner as expected. Notably, the inhibition induced by CDDP was dramatically improved in the presence of GQDs. CDDP (4 nM) alone showed 15% of inhibition; while it was boosted to 60% by adding 8ngmL−1 of GQDs. These results reveal that the presence of GQDs reinforces the inhibition of DNA proliferation that caused by the binding of CDDP to DNA, though CDDP may use its labile ligands to bind to GQDs too. The result suggested that CDDP may also benefit from other advantages of GQDs to improve its interaction with DNA, for instance, the high affinity of GQDs to DNA. These, certainly, need further investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs boost cellular and nuclear uptake of Pt by increasing the cell permeability
ce:section.ce:para: The average lateral size of GQDs used in this work is about 40nm, they enter the cells primarily through caveolae-mediated endocytosis. 20,24 These pathways thus may improve the CDDP cellular uptake if CDDP is transported with GQDs as a complex. Our previous results have also shown that GQDs can easily enter nucleus, 20 thus it is possible nucleus accumulation of platinum can be increased as well. As shown in Table 1 , the platinum inside the cells that pre-treated with GQDs (38.49±0.10ng[Pt]/mg[protein]) exceeds that in the untreated cells (35.66±0.42ng[Pt]/mg [protein]). The MCF-7 cells were then treated with 15μM of CDDP and CDDP/GQDs (15μM /30μgmL−1) for 6h, respectively, the nuclear DNA were isolated and Pt amount was then quantified by ICP-MS. The CDDP-treated cells display 0.610±0.002ng[Pt]/μg[DNA], whereas CDDP/GQDs-treated cells exhibit 0.760±0.002ng[Pt]/μg[DNA], showing a ~25% increase comparing to the CDDP alone (Table 1). Apparently, GQDs facilitate accumulation of CDDP inside cell and nucleus. Increases of platinum inside cell and nucleus suggest that either CDDP/GQDs together enter the cells as proposed earlier, or GQDs improved the cell permeability thus enhance cellular uptake of CDDP. The former may not be a dominant reason, because as mentioned earlier, the complex might be not stable, and the formation of a complex could reduce the interaction of CDDP with DNA, which contradicts to the result of PCR stop assay (Figure 4), where CDDP binds to DNA stronger in the presence of GQDs. Nevertheless, to test the later possibility, we examined the cell permeability with two small dye molecules, PI with a poor ability to penetrate cell membrane, 42 and rhodamine 123 (Rho123) with a good cell membrane penetrating property. 43 As shown in Figure 5 , after the incubation with 50μgmL−1 of GQDs for 6h, MCF-7 cells maintain in a healthy state. After the staining with 10μgmL−1 of PI for 3min, no fluorescence was observed in the untreated cells, indicating that PI molecules did not enter the cells. However, weak red fluorescence was observed in the cells that pre-incubated with GQDs, indicating some PI molecules entered these cells. Similarly, after the staining with 1μM of Rho123 for 5min (much shorter than 30min in the usual protocol), green fluorescence observed in the GQDs pre-incubated cells is stronger than that in the untreated cells. These results indicate that the cell permeability is improved upon the incubation with GQDs, accordingly the cellular uptake of CDDP is improved. The result that the CDDP concentration increases inside the cells in the presence of GQDs, highly agrees with the observation of the increase of the apoptosis body, cell cycle effect, and increase of DNA damage.

We recently found that the cell permeability can be enhanced by the incubation with micrometer sized graphene oxide (GO). 44 Thus, to further solidify the result, cellular uptake of platinum in the GO pretreated cells was measured, and about 2-fold of Pt was found inside the cell comparing to the GQDs treated cells (Table 1). Accordingly, the nuclear fragmentation/condensation induced by CDDP was also improved much more than that of GQDs under the same experimental condition (Figure 6 ). Since under the experimental condition most GO did not enter the cells, the enhancement by GO to the activity of CDDP contributed primarily by the increase of the cellular uptake of CDDP. 43 However, GQDs can enter the cells very easily due to their smaller lateral size, we thus cannot preclude the contribution from the entered GQDs. Apparently, the influence of the GQDS on the cell permeability is a dominant contribution. On the basis of these results, we believe that GQDs enhance the activity of CDDP mainly through the mechanisms as shown in Figure 7 . In addition to the traditional pathways, GQDs enhance the cell permeability primarily, thus increase cellular uptake of CDDP; meanwhile some CDDP/GQD complexes may exist, which enter the cells through different pathways comparing to free CDDP, thus the cellular accumulation and activity of CDDP were improved too. Once CDDP is inside the cells, GQDs might also help to improve the interaction of CDDP with DNA using their high affinity to DNA molecules.

The finding that GQDs enhance the cell permeability of CDDP was also applied to the CDDP resistant cell line A549/DDP. As expected, cellular Pt amount in the GQDs pre-treated A549/DDP cells is much higher than the untreated cells (20.6 vs 39.5ng [Pt]/ mg [protein]), almost 2-fold amount of Pt was found inside the GQDs treated cells. This Pt amount is close to the CDDP concentration inside the non-resistant A549 cells under the same condition (Table 1).

As we previously demonstrated that the interaction of GQDs with copper complexes made the complexes active in DNA cleavage. 21 GQDs also exhibit DNA cleavage activity with copper ions for they can chauffeur metal ions to the site where DNA can interact with. 16 Very recently, we also found that GO could increase the cell permeability. 44 It is thus possible that the combination of GQDs and CDDP makes CDDP entering the cells easily and interacting more efficiently with its primary target DNA. The experimental results with the combination of CDDP and GQDs were as expected, GQDs enhanced the cytotoxicity of CDDP to the different cancerous cell lines (Figure 1). Though the combination of CDDP and GQDs did not enhance the cytotoxicity of CDDP to the tested normal cells (Figure S1), it definitely needs more evidences to conclude that the enhancement of GQDs to CDDP is specific to the cancerous cell lines. The combination of CDDP with GQDs also led to more cells arrested at G2/M phase and severe cell nuclear fragmentation/condensation that generated by CDDP (Figure 2). The interaction between GQDs and CDDP, and with DNA at molecular level was thus investigated in vitro (Figures 3 and 4) to understand these results. The interaction between GQDs and CDDP does exist (Figure 3), however, the CDDP/GQDs complex may not be very stable due to their interaction nature, which is supported by the small shifts in their IR spectra (Figure S4) and enhancement in CDDP binding to DNA (Figure 4). Nevertheless, the improvement in CDDP performance by the combination of GQDs is quite similar to the result of using higher concentration of CDDP (Figure S3), which suggests that it is possible that GQDs simply increase the accumulation of GQDs inside the cells. The higher Pt concentrations inside the GQDs pre-treated cells clearly showed that GQDs could efficiently facilitate the cellular uptake of CDDP (Table 1, Figure 5), hence improve the performance of CDDP (Figure 6). However the mechanism of how GQDs interact with the membrane is not well-understood yet, it might be similar to that proposed for GO. 45 In addition, the small lateral sizes of GQDs made it possible for them to enter the cell nucleus easily, 20 therefore, they could also enhance the interaction of CDDP with DNA by taking the advantage of their high affinity to DNA molecules (Figure 4). The enhancement Pt accumulation in the CDDP resistant A549 cells (A549/CDDP) after pre-incubated with GQDs further confirms the function of GQDs to CDDP cellular uptake, which implies that GQDs might be an effective assistant reagent to improve pharmaceutical performance of the other drugs that encounter drug resistant side effect.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article.
Supplementary figures.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.nano.2016.03.010.
